subject: PHIL
course_description: A small class on topics of sociological concern that fosters a highly interactive and mutually collaborative learning environment among students and between students and teachers.
course_notes: (Freshmen Seminar - Limited to First Year Students Only) The transition from premodern to modern society has been analyzed by every major social theorist from the Enlightenment onward. In this first-year seminar, we will explore several "master trends" associated with this change, including: Rationalization. This refers to a highly general historical process that has permeated all aspects of modern life and which broadly speaking involves the gradual displacement of Tradition by Reason; Individualization. Another very general historical trend, individualism is characterized by a diverse set of ideas and practices that promote the individual over the group as the primary unit of modern society; Secularization. This is a complex set of changes in which religion loses some of its cultural authority to define meaning and purpose; Medicalization. This is the process by which everyday experiences and conditions come to be defined as medical problems requiring treatment; Globalization. This is an ancient process that has accelerated in the modern era as various kinds of international connections (economic, political, cultural) develop and multiply. We will discuss a wide range of specific topics related to these trends, including why McDonalds is a model of bureaucratic organization; how legal due process and political democracy came to be seen as basic human rights; why fashion is paradoxically a sign of both self-expression and group conformity; how views of deviant behavior have evolved over time, from sin to crime to sickness; your morning cup of coffee as a global supply chain; and terrorism as backlash against the forces of globalization and secularization.
requisites: Two PHIL courses or equivalent transfer credit as prerequisite.
